main_theme: An introduction to the evolution of mobile network technologies and generations.
sub_topics: Cellular system fundamentals (reuse, handoff, power control)
Rationale for cellular systems (coverage and capacity)
Multiple access techniques (FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, OFDMA)
Radio and core network architecture across generations
Interpretation of 'G' labels and their relation to standards
Connection between network design and real-world applications (voice, data, IoT)

claims: Cellular systems employ frequency reuse.
Handoff is a fundamental aspect of cellular systems.
Power control is a fundamental aspect of cellular systems.

claims_by_chunk.topic: Mobile Network Generations and Access Techniques
claims: Cellular systems are organized into cells to provide coverage and capacity.
Multiple access techniques have evolved from FDMA to TDMA/CDMA to OFDMA across mobile network generations.
2G, 3G, 4G, 5G, and 6G networks each have distinct radio and core network blocks.
Cellular systems are organized into cells to improve coverage and capacity.
Mobile network generations have evolved from 1G to 5G.
2G mobile networks utilized GSM and CDMA technologies.
3G mobile networks utilized UMTS and WCDMA technologies.
4G mobile networks utilized LTE technology.
5G mobile networks utilize NR and 5GC technologies.
6G mobile networks are heading towards utilizing Terahertz frequencies.
Traffic demand grows from voice to messaging to smartphone broadband to massive IoT.
Spectrum is a scarce resource, necessitating better spectral efficiency and reuse.
Mobility and reliability requirements increase with each generation, including handoff, QoS, and security.
Core networks evolve from circuit switching to packet switching to cloud-native services.
Each mobile network generation includes changes to radio access, such as waveform, multiple access, coding, and MIMO.
Each mobile network generation includes changes to the core network, such as switching, mobility management, and policy/QoS.
New services, such as SMS, mobile web, streaming, and URLLC, become standard with each generation.
Marketing labels for mobile network generations can differ from strict ITU definitions.
A cellular network splits a region into multiple small coverage areas called cells.
Cells are served by low-power base stations.
The same frequencies can be reused in non-adjacent cells to increase capacity.
Handoff allows users to move between cells while maintaining connectivity.
Capacity can be scaled by densifying the network with smaller cells and more sites.
Smarter interference control can improve network capacity.
A base station consists of an antenna, radios, and control functions serving a cell.
The core network handles switching, routing, mobility management, and subscriber databases.
Control channels are used for setup, paging, and mobility management.
Traffic channels are used for user voice and data transmission.
BTS is the naming convention for 2G radio sites.
NodeB is the naming convention for 3G radio sites.
eNodeB is the naming convention for 4G radio sites.
gNodeB is the naming convention for 5G radio sites.
A traversing wave exists in cellular network organization.
A reflected wave exists in cellular network organization.
Real cellular coverage is irregular due to terrain and buildings.
Cellular network planning uses idealized shapes for coverage modeling.
Hexagons approximate equal-distance neighbors in cellular networks.
Hexagons simplify frequency reuse planning in cellular networks.
The cell radius influences cellular coverage.
The inter-site distance influences cellular coverage.
The cell radius influences interference in cellular networks.
The inter-site distance influences interference in cellular networks.
Square cell patterns have a distance of √3.
Adjacent cells in a mobile network use different channels to limit co-channel interference.
A cluster of N cells uses a complete set of frequencies and then this pattern repeats.
The reuse factor is calculated as 1/N, where N is the number of cells in a cluster.
A smaller reuse factor (N) indicates more frequency reuse and a greater need for interference control.
There is a trade-off between capacity and interference in mobile networks.
Signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) and quality are affected by the capacity versus interference trade-off.
A signal strength of less than 0 dB indicates a very poor signal and high interference/noise.
A signal strength between 0 and 10 dB results in a low, unstable connection.
A signal strength between 10 and 20 dB provides a moderate to good connection.
A signal strength greater than 20 dB provides an excellent connection and high throughput.
An RSRP (dBm) value greater than or equal to -10 dBm indicates excellent signal strength.
An RSRQ (dB) value greater than or equal to -10 dB indicates excellent signal strength.
A SINR (dB) value greater than or equal to 20 dB indicates excellent signal strength.
An RSRP (dBm) value between -80 and -90 dBm corresponds to an RSRQ (dB) value between -10 and -15 dB and a SINR (dB) value between 13 and 20 dB.
An RSRP (dBm) value between -90 and -100 dBm corresponds to an RSRQ (dB) value between -15 and -20 dB and a SINR (dB) value between 0 and 13 dB.
An RSRP (dBm) value less than or equal to -100 dBm corresponds to an RSRQ (dB) value less than or equal to -20 dB and a SINR (dB) value less than or equal to 0 dB.
The reuse distance D increases with cluster size N.
A frequency reuse pattern can be represented by a circle with radius D.
Planning involves choosing N to balance capacity.
The equation N = 2 + + (I × ) represents a relationship between cluster size and other factors.
According to the Law of Cosines, D = √3N.
D represents the minimum frequency reuse distance.
R represents the cell radius.
Modern systems incorporate interference coordination and dynamic reuse.
The reuse factor is equal to 1/N.
The ratio D/R depends on the cluster size N.
Reuse distance D grows with cluster size N.
The formula D = √3N calculates the minimum frequency reuse distance.
R represents the cell radius.
1/N represents the reuse factor.
Modern systems utilize interference coordination and dynamic reuse.
Adding spectrum and channels can increase network capacity.
Cell splitting involves using smaller cells in areas with high traffic.
Sectoring utilizes directional antennas to reduce interference.
Three sectors per site is an example of sectoring.
Densification involves using microcells and femtocells.
Antennas can be placed on rooftops and lamp posts for densification.
Interference coordination enables tighter frequency reuse.
Interference Coordination (ICIC) and Coordinated Multi-Point (CoMP) are used in LTE/LTE-A networks.
Interference Coordination (ICIC) reduces inter-cell interference (ICI).
Interference Coordination (ICIC) uses frequency and power coordination via X2 interfaces.
Coordinated Multi-Point (CoMP) allows multiple base stations to coordinate transmissions.
Coordinated Multi-Point (CoMP) offers dynamic management for higher data rates.
Cell splitting involves replacing one large cell with multiple smaller cells.
Cell splitting increases capacity by reusing spectrum more frequently.
Cell splitting requires more sites.
Cell splitting requires more fronthaul infrastructure.
Cell splitting requires more backhaul infrastructure.
Cell splitting requires increased coordination.
Fronthaul and backhaul are distinct segments in mobile network architecture.
Fronthaul and backhaul connect user devices to the core network.
Cell splitting increases spectrum reuse in high-demand areas.
Sectoring divides a cell into wedge-shaped sectors using directional antennas.
Microcells are small outdoor cells designed for dense urban streets.
Femtocells are very small indoor cells intended for use in homes or enterprises.
Multiple microcells and picocells can be used to increase network capacity.
Special tuning can be applied to manage uplink interference.
A multi-layer network design is called a HetNet (heterogeneous network).
The goal of sectoring, microcells, and femtocells is to improve Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR).
The goal of sectoring, microcells, and femtocells is to increase spatial reuse.
Control channels in a mobile network carry synchronization information.
Control channels in a mobile network carry system information.
Control channels in a mobile network carry paging messages.
Control channels in a mobile network carry access requests.
Paging in a mobile network is a process used to locate and establish communication with a mobile device when it is not in active use.
Paging in a mobile network is used when a mobile device needs to be reached for an incoming call.
Paging in a mobile network is used when a mobile device needs to be reached for an incoming message.
Paging in a mobile network is used when a mobile device needs to be reached for an incoming data session.
Traffic channels carry user voice frames.
Traffic channels carry packet data.
Capacity in a mobile network is limited by data resources.
Capacity in a mobile network is limited by control overhead.
5G adds richer control for beam management.
5G adds control for ultra-reliable access.
User equipment (UE) connects to a base station over the air interface.
Base stations connect to the core network, which handles switching, routing, and subscriber services.
The core network connects to the Internet and public telephone networks.
Handoff transfers an active connection as a User Equipment (UE) moves between cells.
Hard handoff is a break-before-make connection transfer.
Hard handoff is typical in GSM, LTE, and NR systems.
Soft handoff is a make-before-break connection transfer.
Soft handoff was classic in CDMA systems.
The goal of handoff is to minimize call drop and interruption.
Handoff aims to control interference.
Handoff transfers an active connection as a User Equipment (UE) moves between cells.
Hard handoff is a break-before-make process, typical in GSM, LTE, and NR.
Soft handoff is a make-before-break process, classic in CDMA systems.
A goal of handoff is to minimize call drop and interruption while controlling interference.
Handoff decisions can be based on comparisons of relative signal strength between serving and neighbor cells.
Threshold Th1 prevents 'ping-pong' effects on weak edges.
Threshold Th2 is assigned to base station B.
Handoff to base station A occurs when a handoff to base station B is initiated.
A car is moving from base station A at location L to base station B at location Lβ.
The goal of power control is to provide enough power for reliable reception without causing excessive interference.
Open-loop power control involves the User Equipment (UE) estimating path loss and setting power.
Open-loop power control is fast but less accurate.
Closed-loop power control involves the network commanding UE power adjustments.
Closed-loop power control is accurate but requires feedback.
Power control is critical in Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA) systems.
Power control is critical in uplink-limited systems.
The near–far problem is addressed by power control in CDMA and uplink-limited systems.
Systems have a finite number of channels and resources.
Some calls or requests may be blocked when systems are under load.
Grade of Service (GoS) is the probability of blocking or dropping calls.
Grade of Service (GoS) is a key metric in telecommunications and contact centres.
Erlang models estimate the needed capacity for a given load and blocking probability.
Queueing delay and scheduling are concepts similar to blocking probability in packet systems.
Different tracks of cellular system development evolved in parallel.
GSM evolved to WCDMA.
AMPS evolved to IS-95.
IS-95 evolved to CDMA2000.
CDMA2000 evolved to 1XEV-DO.
1XEV-DO evolved to IXEV-DO.
WCDMA evolved to LTE.
WCDMA evolved to LTE-Advanced.
IXEV-DO remained at IXEV-DO.
Rel.0 evolved to Rel.A.
Rel.A evolved to Rev. B.
Each generation of mobile networks improves data rates.
Each generation of mobile networks improves spectral efficiency.
The 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) evolves standards in releases.
1G evolved to 2G.
2G evolved to 3G.
3G evolved to LTE.
LTE evolved to LTE-Advanced.
Data rates in the first generation (1G) were less than or equal to 10 kbps.
Data rates in 2G were 9.664 xops.
Data rates in 3G were 384 ktps-2 M0ps.
Data rates in 3.9G were 384 kbps.
Data rates in 4G were 20 Mbps.
Data rates in later 4G networks reached <100 Mbps.
Data rates in advanced 4G networks exceeded 100 Mbps.
1G networks, introduced in the 1980s, utilized analog voice transmission with Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA).
1G networks provided voice call services.
2G networks, introduced in the 1990s, supported digital voice and Short Message Service (SMS).
2G networks provided voice, SMS, and low-rate data services.
2.5G networks, introduced in the 2000s, enabled packet data transmission on 2G cores.
2.5G networks provided Wireless Application Protocol (WAP), email, and early web access.
3G networks, introduced in the 2000s, were based on the International Mobile Telecommunications 2000 (IMT-2000) standard.
3G networks provided web access, multimedia services, and video calling.
4G networks, introduced in the 2010s, were based on an All-IP broadband architecture using Long Term Evolution (LTE).
4G networks provided streaming, applications, and Voice over IP (VoIP) services.
5G networks, introduced in the 2020s, utilize a flexible New Radio (NR) interface and a 5G core network.
5G networks support enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB), Ultra-Reliable Low Latency Communications (URLLC), and massive Internet of Things (IoT).
1G mobile networks utilized analog voice transmission with Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA).
1G mobile networks provided voice calls as a typical service.
The first commercial cellular systems were introduced in the 1980s.
1G used analog FM for voice transmission.
1G utilized circuit-switched calls.
1G employed FDMA with relatively wide channels.
AMPS used 30 kHz channels in 1G.
1G had limited security.
1G had limited capacity, creating pressure for digital systems.
AMPS was a 1G standard used in North America.
NMT was a 1G standard used in Nordic countries.
TACS was a 1G standard used in the UK and Europe.
1G systems used Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD) operation with separate uplink and downlink frequency bands.
In 1G systems, each call occupied one dedicated frequency channel.
Roaming and interoperability were limited across regions in 1G networks.
AMPS used analog voice using Frequency Modulation (FM).
AMPS operated around the 800 MHz band.
AMPS utilized Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA) where one user is allocated per frequency channel.
AMPS had a 30 kHz channel spacing.
AMPS employed control channels for paging and call setup.
AMPS employed traffic channels for voice.
AMPS used separate uplink and downlink bands with Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD).
AMPS planned frequency reuse via clusters.
AMPS allocated one dedicated analog channel per call.
The first generation of mobile networks (1G) featured large handsets.
The first generation of mobile networks (1G) had expensive minutes.
The first generation of mobile networks (1G) provided limited coverage.
The second generation of mobile networks (2G) enabled smaller devices.
The second generation of mobile networks (2G) introduced SMS functionality.
The second generation of mobile networks (2G) offered improved battery life.
The third, fourth, and fifth generations of mobile networks (3G, 4G, 5G) facilitated smartphone usage.
The third, fourth, and fifth generations of mobile networks (3G, 4G, 5G) supported data-centric usage.
The Nokia 10108 device was associated with the first generation of mobile networks (1G) in 1981.
The second generation of mobile networks (2G) became available in 1992.
The third generation of mobile networks (3G) became available in 2001.
The fourth generation of mobile networks (4G) became available in 2011.
The fifth generation of mobile networks (5G) became available in 2020.
1G systems had capacity limits due to one channel per call.
1G systems experienced interference constraints.
Audio quality in 1G degraded with noise.
Audio quality in 1G degraded with fading.
1G analog calls were easy to eavesdrop on due to weak security.
1G did not have efficient packet data support.
2G networks provide digital voice services.
2G networks provide messaging services.
2G networks offer improved capacity compared to 1G networks.
2G networks represent a shift from analog to digital traffic channels.
TDMA (GSM) is a method for achieving better spectral efficiency in 2G networks.
CDMA (IS-95) is a method for achieving better spectral efficiency in 2G networks.
Encryption in 2G networks improves privacy.
Error correction in 2G networks improves voice quality.
SMS was enabled by 2G networks.
Basic data services were enabled by 2G networks.

claims_by_chunk.topic: Multiple Access Techniques in Mobile Networks
claims: FDMA splits the spectrum into frequency channels, as used in 1G networks.
TDMA shares a frequency by dividing it into time slots.
GSM utilizes TDMA, allowing 8 users per 200 kHz carrier.
CDMA shares time and frequency using spreading codes to separate users.
OFDMA shares many narrow subcarriers dynamically.
LTE and NR utilize OFDMA.
GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) was originally developed for Europe.
GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) is now deployed globally.
GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) uses SIM-based identity to enable device portability.
SIMs used in GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) networks can roam.
GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) combines FDMA for carriers with TDMA for time slots.
GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) defines both the radio interface and core network functions.
The GSM architecture includes both a radio interface and a core network.
BTS (cell) is controlled by BSC (handoff within BSS) in the GSM radio access network.
AuC, HLR, VLR, and EIR are subscriber databases within the GSM core network.
The ME (Mobile Equipment) communicates with the Base transceiver station.
GPRS nodes (SGSN/GGSN) were later added to GSM for packet data.
The Um interface connects the ME to the Base transceiver station.
The Abis interface connects the Base transceiver station to the BSC.
The VLR is a component of the GSM network.
The HLR is a component of the GSM network.
The Base station is a component of the GSM network.
The Mobile Operation and maintenance center is a component of the GSM network.
The carrier spacing in GSM is 200 kHz.
Each carrier in GSM supports 8 time slots within a TDMA frame.
Voice in GSM is encoded using a speech codec.
Voice in GSM is protected by error control coding.
Interleaving in GSM helps mitigate burst errors caused by fading.
A Hyperframe in GSM consists of 2048 Superframes.
GMSK modulation is used in classic GSM.
EDGE uses 8-PSK modulation.
A Multiframe in GSM consists of 26 Multiframe.
A Superframe in GSM consists of 51 Multiframe.
A Superframe in GSM is approximately 235 milliseconds in duration.
A TDMA Frame in GSM is 4.615 milliseconds in duration.
The SIM in GSM contains secret key material.
The GSM network authenticates the SIM via a challenge-response mechanism.
Air-interface encryption in GSM protects voice and signaling over the radio link.
Temporary identifiers, such as TMSI, help reduce tracking in GSM.
Security in GSM is strongest on the radio hop.
End-to-end security in GSM is application-dependent.
Circuit-switched data is inefficient for bursty internet traffic.
GPRS introduces packet switching.
GPRS provides "always-on" connectivity.
EDGE increases bits per symbol using 8-PSK modulation.
EDGE provides higher data rates than previous generations.
IS-95 (cdmaOne) is based on DS-CDMA (spread spectrum).
In IS-95 (cdmaOne), many users share the same wideband carrier.
Users in IS-95 (cdmaOne) are separated by codes.
IS-95 (cdmaOne) requires tight power control to avoid near–far problems.
IS-95 (cdmaOne) supports soft handoff.
IS-95 (cdmaOne) utilizes RAKE receivers for multipath combining.
Wideband spreading in CDMA reduces the impact of frequency-selective fading.
RAKE combines energy from multiple signal paths.
CDMA experiences graceful degradation as the number of users increases interference gradually.
Power control is mandatory in CDMA to address the near–far problem.
Uncontrolled users in CDMA can cause self-jamming.
CDMA capacity is limited by interference and required quality, not just the number of channels.
A User Equipment (UE) can connect to multiple base stations briefly during transitions using soft handoff.
A RAKE receiver correlates multiple delayed copies of a signal.
A RAKE receiver combines multiple delayed copies of a signal.
Soft handoff and a RAKE receiver provide smoother mobility.
Soft handoff and a RAKE receiver improve link robustness.
Soft handoff increases signaling within the network.
Soft handoff increases complexity in the User Equipment (UE).
2G networks provided clearer voice quality through the use of digital codecs and error correction.
SMS and early messaging ecosystems were introduced with 2G networks.
Roaming became practical with 2G networks, especially with GSM SIMs.
Early mobile data rates in 2G networks reached tens of kbps using CSD, and later GPRS/EDGE.
GPRS and EDGE utilize the foundations of 2G networks for packet data transmission.
GPRS adds packet-switched data to GSM Phase 2.
GPRS supports persistent data connections.
Persistent data connections are better for bursty traffic.
GPRS introduces packet core nodes called SGSN.
GPRS introduces packet core nodes called GGSN.
GPRS introduces new Quality of Service (QoS) concepts.
GPRS throughput depends on coding.
GPRS throughput depends on the number of time slots assigned.
EDGE utilizes higher-order modulation, such as 8-PSK, to increase the number of bits per symbol.
EDGE is often referred to as “2.75G” due to its data rates approaching early 3G.
EDGE is compatible with GSM carriers and time slots, representing an incremental upgrade.
EDGE enabled improved WAP and web experiences prior to the widespread adoption of 3G wideband technology.
3G networks aimed to provide higher data rates for internet access.
3G networks aimed to provide higher data rates for multimedia applications.
3G networks aimed to support global roaming.
3G networks aimed to provide service continuity.
3G networks were designed to support both circuit-switched voice communication.
3G networks were designed to support packet-switched data communication.
3G networks aimed for better spectral efficiency.
3G networks aimed to improve mobility support.
UMTS / WCDMA is an evolution path for GSM operators.
UMTS / WCDMA was standardized in 3GPP.
CDMA2000 is an evolution path for IS-95.
CDMA2000 historically existed within the 3GPP2 ecosystem.
TD-SCDMA is a time-division synchronous CDMA technology.
TD-SCDMA was historically used in China.
Many networks later evolved toward LTE as a common 4G path.
In a 3G operating environment, cell size varies by environment.
GEO satellites utilize macro, micro, and pico cells, with pico cells being for indoor use.
GEO satellites also utilize Mega Cells which are regional.
GEO satellites also utilize Micro Cells which are for urban areas.
LEO/MEO satellites utilize Global Cells.
LEO/MEO satellites utilize Macro Cells which are for suburban areas.
LEO/MEO satellites utilize Pico Cells which are for in-building use.
Smaller cells enable higher capacity.
Smaller cells enable better indoor coverage.
Satellite layers can complement terrestrial coverage.
The Radio Network Subsystem (RNS) includes the Radio Network Controller (RNC) and the User Equipment (UE).
The Radio Network Controller (RNC) handles radio resource control.
The Radio Network Controller (RNC) handles mobility decisions.
Node B is the 3G base station.
Node B supports Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD).
Node B supports Time Division Duplexing (TDD).
Node B can support both FDD and TDD.
The Radio Network Controller (RNC) is responsible for handover decisions requiring signaling to the User Equipment (UE).
A cell offers Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD).
A cell offers Time Division Duplexing (TDD).
The circuit-switched domain in UMTS supports traditional voice calling using MSC/VLR.
The packet-switched domain in UMTS supports data using SGSN/GGSN from GPRS.
Release 99 of UMTS reused much of the GSM/GPRS core network to accelerate deployment.
Later releases of UMTS enhanced packet capability with HSPA/HSPA+.
Evolved High Speed Packet Access (HSPA+) is a technical standard for wireless broadband telecommunication.
HSPA+ is an evolution of the earlier HSPA standard.
Wideband CDMA uses a constant chip rate.
Different spreading factors in Wideband CDMA support different data rates.
OVSF coding is used in Wideband CDMA.
Scrambling codes separate users in WCDMA.
Scrambling codes relax strict synchronization requirements in WCDMA.
HSDPA utilizes adaptive modulation and coding techniques.
HSDPA utilizes HARQ (Hybrid Automatic Repeat Request).
HSDPA utilizes fast scheduling.
HSUPA provides higher uplink throughput.
HSUPA improves latency.
HSPA+ employs higher-order modulation, such as 64-QAM.
HSPA+ utilizes MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output).
HSPA+ utilizes multi-carrier technology.
HSDPA is a downlink technology.
HSUPA is an uplink technology.
HSDPA, HSUPA, and HSPA+ are considered 'evolved 3G' systems.
Evolved 3G systems bridged toward LTE capabilities.
In CDMA systems, an increase in the number of users leads to increased interference.
Increased interference in CDMA systems results in reduced coverage.
In GSM, a mobile device receives an exclusive signal from the base station.
In GSM, the number of devices in a cell does not influence cell size.
In UMTS, cell size is closely correlated to cell capacity.
Signal-to-noise ratio determines cell capacity in UMTS.
Noise in UMTS is generated by interference from other cells.
Noise in UMTS is generated by interference from other users of the same cell.
Edge users in UMTS may drop out of the cell when they hit power limits.
Dropping of edge users in UMTS shrinks the effective cell size.
Interference increases the noise level in UMTS.
Devices at the edge of a UMTS cell cannot increase their output power beyond a maximum limit.
A limitation on the maximum number of users within a cell is required in UMTS.
Cell breathing complicates network planning in UMTS.
Network planning in UMTS must account for load-dependent coverage.
In UMTS, capacity and coverage are linked through cell breathing.
3G systems enabled internet access on mobile phones.
3G systems utilized packet data and shared radio resources dynamically.
Users of 3G systems experienced variable throughput.
Users of 3G systems experienced variable latency.
Early video calling in 3G systems was limited by uplink capacity.
Early video calling in 3G systems was limited by latency.
Mobile web browsing speeds in 3G ranged from hundreds of kbps to a few Mbps.
3G systems supported picture messaging and richer media.
The variable throughput and latency of 3G motivated improvements in scheduling.
The variable throughput and latency of 3G motivated the move to OFDMA in LTE.
4G systems utilize an All-IP Broadband architecture.
LTE and LTE-Advanced are examples of 4G technologies.
4G utilizes an All-IP packet core.
Voice in 4G is implemented as VoIP over LTE, known as VoLTE.
LTE employs OFDMA for the downlink.
LTE employs SC-FDMA for the uplink.
4G networks exhibit lower latency compared to previous generations.
4G networks have higher spectral efficiency than previous generations.
MIMO and improved coding techniques contribute to the higher spectral efficiency of 4G.
4G networks feature a flat architecture.
More functionality was moved to the base station (eNodeB) in 4G networks.
4G (IMT-Advanced) had a peak data rate target of approximately 100 Mbit/s for high mobility scenarios.
4G (IMT-Advanced) had a peak data rate target of approximately 1 Gbit/s for low mobility scenarios.
4G networks utilized an all-IP packet switched network architecture.
4G networks aimed for scalable bandwidth.
4G networks aimed for high spectral efficiency.
Initial LTE deployments were marketed as 4G before fully meeting IMT-Advanced targets.
LTE-Advanced introduced carrier aggregation features.
LTE-Advanced features aligned better with IMT-Advanced expectations.
The E-UTRAN utilizes eNodeB base stations to handle radio scheduling and mobility.
The S1-MME interface connects to the iCore.c component.
The LTE-Uu interface connects to the UE (User Equipment).
The Evolved Packet Core (EPC) includes the MME for control functions.
The Evolved Packet Core (EPC) includes SGW/PGW for user plane gateway functions.
The Evolved Packet Core (EPC) includes the HSS for subscriber data.
Bearers provide Quality of Service (QoS).
Policy control steers traffic and charging.
VoLTE utilizes IMS for voice services over IP.
Downlink in LTE uses Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (OFDMA) with many narrow subcarriers.
LTE schedulers assign time-frequency resource blocks to each user.
Adaptive modulation and coding (AMC) in LTE reacts to channel quality.
A Resource Block (RB) is the smallest schedulable unit in LTE.
Hybrid Automatic Repeat Request (HARQ) provides fast retransmissions in LTE.
Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) uses multiple antennas to improve throughput and coverage in LTE.
Uplink in LTE uses Single-Carrier Frequency Division Multiple Access (SC-FDMA) to reduce User Equipment (UE) power amplifier stress.
LTE-Advanced utilizes carrier aggregation to combine multiple carriers for wider bandwidth.
LTE-Advanced employs higher-order MIMO configurations, such as 4x4 and 8x8 in some cases.
CoMP and interference coordination in LTE-Advanced improve performance at the edge of the network.
HetNets, which mix macro cells and small cells, are used in LTE-Advanced for coverage and capacity.
Voice over LTE (VoLTE) implements voice as an IP service (VoIP) on the LTE packet core.
VoLTE provides better spectral efficiency than legacy circuit-switched voice.
VoLTE enables high-definition voice quality.
VoLTE enables faster call setup times.
VoLTE requires an IP Multimedia Subsystem (IMS) for operation.
VoLTE requires Quality of Service (QoS) mechanisms.
VoLTE requires careful interworking mechanisms to support roaming.
A phone call on LTE is a real-time IP session with strict delay and loss requirements.
Networks prioritize VoLTE traffic using dedicated QoS bearers.
Other applications share best-effort resources when VoLTE is in use.
LTE control channels include scheduling metadata that is often unencrypted.
LTEye demonstrates low-cost passive monitoring to infer utilization and link quality in LTE networks.
LTEye can help researchers understand spectrum use.
LTEye can help policy makers understand spectrum use.
LTEye can help researchers understand LTE network performance.
LTEye can help policy makers understand LTE network performance.
Radio scheduling shapes user experience in LTE networks.
Cellular links exhibit highly variable capacity due to scheduling and channel changes.
Classic congestion control mechanisms may cause per-user queues to become overfilled, resulting in high latency.
Sprout forecasts near-future delivery to minimize delay while sustaining throughput.
Radio evolution necessitates changes in the behavior of end-to-end protocols.
5G utilizes New Radio (NR) and a 5G Core network.
5G supports enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB).
5G supports Ultra-Reliable Low Latency Communications (URLLC).
5G supports massive Internet of Things (IoT).
Mobile data is experiencing an explosion in volume due to video and cloud applications.
There is a need for better efficiency in mobile networks.
Industrial automation is a new vertical application for 5G networks.
Connected vehicles are a new vertical application for 5G networks.
Smart cities are a new vertical application for 5G networks.
Massive IoT requires high device density.
Massive IoT requires low energy operation.
5G networks are designed to be more flexible.
Network slicing is a feature of 5G networks.
Edge computing is a feature of 5G networks.
Private deployments are a feature of 5G networks.
The peak data rate target for 5G downlink is 20 Gbit/s.
The peak data rate target for 5G uplink is 10 Gbit/s.
The user experienced data rate for 5G is 100 Mbit/s downlink in typical coverage.
The latency target for 5G URLLC user plane is approximately 1 ms.
The latency target for 5G eMBB user plane is approximately 4 ms.
The connection density for 5G mMTC can reach up to 1,000,000 devices per km² under specific scenarios.

Real 5G performance depends on bandwidth.

6G networks are expected to be 100 times faster than 5G networks.
